title: Why the Next Wave of Enterprise Strategy is Industry Clouds
body_paragraphs: Cloud transformation is on course to generate as much as $3 trillion by 2030. Key to this shift is the emergence of artificial intelligence (AI)-enabled industry clouds, also called industry suites, that marry core infrastructure and applications with regulatory considerations to built-in industry-specific context, process capabilities, and workflows. These include health care industry clouds with embedded compliance and AI diagnostic tools, industry banking clouds with fraud detection and risk analytics, and utility industry clouds geared to support smart grid data and Internet of Things sensors.

Beyond workforce collaboration, analytics-driven real-time decision making, and other baseline improvements, AI-enabled industry clouds help enterprises access better insights, cut IT costs, and design more resilient and adaptive business models that are becoming increasingly essential to compete. The percentage of enterprises using industry-specific cloud platforms will rise to 70% by 2027 from just 15% in 2023, according to Gartner.
Many organizations remain in the early stages of scaling AI across enterprise operations, according to McKinsey research.

“AI is no longer a future bet—it’s a present-day business driver,” says Tim Hatton, VP–Strategic Partner Management at Oracle. “Organizations are moving beyond isolated AI experiments and embedding AI into core operational processes. The focus is increasingly on measurable results, operational efficiency, and faster time to value.”

“The market is clearly at an inflection point as enterprises move from AI experimentation to scaled adoption, with the focus shifting decisively to measurable outcomes,” says Ravi Seshadhri, Vice President and Global Head of Oracle Business at Wipro. “I’m seeing organizations embrace embedded AI in core business functions to realize value at scale.”
Turning Investments into Outcomes
For enterprise resource planning (ERP) and IT leaders, specialized AI capabilities offer value beyond standard embedded AI capabilities.

This shift toward AI-enabled industry clouds is helping these leaders reimagine industry-specific workflows, such as patient management and billing cycles, with AI at the core. This evolution is also transforming software development life cycles to help enterprises identify pain points in discovery and design, compress cycle times and reduce subject matter expert dependence through the build and configure phases, automate test cases and predictions, and drive productivity gains by using AI agents for analytics, ticket resolution, and continuous improvement.

Upgrading cloud technology and implementation and transformation expertise can help cut costs and drive growth in various sectors. One Japan-based global automotive components manufacturer says it cut how-to and error-related tickets by 40% by introducing human capital management agents. A multinational energy company based in Canada adopted procurement and customer experience (CX) agents and says it cut its purchase requisition cycle time by 20%.
The power of combining this technology and expertise is apparent in sectors as diverse as logistics, utilities, and health care.
For global logistics giant DHL, the prospect of cloud transformation meant a technological challenge and complex legal, regulatory, and cultural issues.

To coordinate its financial ERP workflows across 49 markets, each with its own laws, languages, and ways of working, DHL brought in both powerful software-as-a-service (SaaS) applications and a partner with a wide range of in-country knowledge and logistics expertise.
With the support of this external sector- and market-specific cloud expertise, DHL says the transformation helped accelerate decision making processes by 20% while reducing operational costs by 30%.

In its recent drive to modernize its customer relationship management, billing, and service bureau functions, Greater Cincinnati Water Works (GCWW) strengthened its scalability, CX, workforce training, and adoption by migrating its outdated on-premises customer information system to a customer cloud service.

With this upgrade, GCWW says it automated 30% of its billing and customer management processes while increasing paperless billing adoption by 50%. It also transitioned to a cost-saving cloud subscription model.
King’s College Hospital Jeddah in Saudi Arabia struggled with compartmentalized clinical and finance systems that led to slow and error-prone financial closures.

Using an accelerated approach that took just over two months, the hospital implemented SaaS applications for finance, HR, and the supply chain and used Oracle Cloud Infrastructure to build a doctor payout module and integrate data flows from electronic medical records to the applications.

With that upgrade and health care expertise during implementation, the hospital says it cut its financial close time from 10 days to two, reduced the costs of using hardware and manual efforts by 20%, and improved staff morale.
Specialized AI Cloud Expertise

Even with the will and means to apply AI-powered industry-specific cloud technology to business strategy and decision making, an enterprise may face long implementation timelines, data migration issues, inefficient processes, underused capabilities, data management challenges, and other headwinds to adoption. A banking chief information officer may need to ensure AI cloud deployments comply with evolving regulatory frameworks for data sovereignty and algorithmic fairness, while a retail chief technology officer may prioritize minimizing disruption to customer-facing systems during migration. Understanding persona-specific hurdles is critical to designing an effective AI and cloud strategy.

Organizations that can’t yet manage this transformation alone are looking to implementation partners to accelerate deployment, tailor workflows, and scale adoption across business functions to meet operational and financial objectives. The right partnership can guide an enterprise on its AI and cloud journey from discovery and ideation through rapid prototyping and production deployment, using data, security, workflows, and extensibility frameworks to implement enterprise-grade governance and scalability.

At the same time, outside expertise may help alleviate any workforce concerns about the transformative power of this technology, building skill sets and mindsets while navigating legal, regulatory, and CX questions across markets and regions.

Industry-specific AI clouds are increasingly vital to business. By 2028, these platforms will power more than half of enterprises’ business initiatives, Gartner predicts. These platforms’ capabilities and insights are already critical to compete—and establishing a robust AI cloud partnership could be a key factor in determining which enterprises lead their sectors into the future.

Oracle offers integrated suites of applications plus secure, autonomous infrastructure in the Oracle Cloud. Wipro is a leading AI‑powered technology services and consulting company. Learn how Oracle and Wipro can help your organization translate AI ambition into measurable ROI by bringing Wipro Intelligence™ into the Oracle ecosystem to reimagine how AI is embedded across core business processes.
